• Term: Concordance is most frequent in the 8th Term (665, 43.98, 121.58%), but it is most representative in Term 6 (465, 49.52, 136.87%). The raw and relative frequency decrease in the earlier Terms.
• Speaker_role: the concordance is (unsurprisingly) mostly present in speeches made by regular speakers, with only limited amount of instances within chairperson speeches (regular: 2,919, 38.93, 107.60%; chairperson: 36; 5.38, 14.87%). 
• Speaker_gender: The concordance is much more frequently used in speeches, produces by male speaker (2,204, 33.97, 93.90%), but it is more representative of speeches, made by female speakers (751, 44.69, 123.53%)
• Speaker_party: The concordance is most frequently present in speeches made by larger and more present parties (SDS: 847, 42.64, 117.88%; SD: 346, 36.10, 99.78%; NSI: 202, 32.31, 89.32%) and speakers without political party affiliation (guests, 286, 33.78, 90.66%). In terms of relative frequency the concordance is most representative in speeches, made by PS (154, 100.70, 290.21%), LMŠ (119, 61.80, 170.82%) and ZaAB (10, 61.72, 170.62%), which have low(er) raw frequencies, and are parties, that were present in the Parliament for a shorter period of time.

column14: Broad naš–vaš usage reflects Us vs. Them framing, often tied to political polarisation, either to reject division ("Delitev na vaše in naše , delitev na leve in desne... – to se ne bi smelo dogajati." / "The division between yours and ours, the division between left and right - this simply cannot, should not, happen") or to attack the opposing side ("Ampak ti primeri niso iz našega mandata, so iz vašega mandata" / "But these examples are not from our mandate, they are from your mandate."). Many cases do  not necessarily denote political polarisation context, but can be seen as a marker of political identities, e.g. ("naša dolžnost je"/ "our duty is"; "naši in vaši otroci" / "our and your children"...).This link to political polarisation is also evident in the most common collocations.
column18: naš[-i, -e, -ega...] [nam niso nikoli lagali, medtem ko ...] vaš[-i, -e, -ega...]

column14: Concordances include various forms of naš[-i,-ih,-e,...][-, in, pa]vaš[-i, -ih,-e], with most instances explicitly invoking naši–vaši in a polarized political context. Others highlight shared responsibility (e.g., "Slovensko visoko šolstvo je naša in vaša , pa tudi splošna javna odgovornost." / "Slovenian higher education is our responsibility and yours, as well as a general public responsibility"), or express contrast/distancing (e.g., " Za razliko od vaših, so naši [dokumenti] javno objavljeni na spletni strani" / "Unlike yours, our [documents] are publicly available on our website"). The collocations refer mostly to different forms of (binary) division.

column14: Terms, almost explicitly relating to the polarising naši-vaši concept, expressing different binary divisions (e.g. "Pa prenehajmo, za božjo voljo, s temi naši in vaši!"; In ne govoriti, če so naši ali vaši , jaz osebno, [...] ne delam razlik med črnimi, zelenimi, rdečimi, belimi, ne vem, našimi, vašimi, levimi, desnimi."/"Let's stop, for God's sake, with these ours and yours!"; And let's not talk about whether they are ours or yours , I personally, [...] I don't distinguish between black, green, red, white, I don't know, ours, yours, left, right"). However, there are some notable instances (< 5), where the term relates to shared values (naši, vaši volilci, otroci...). Given the amount of punctuation identified in the collocations, we also filtered out the patterns where the words in question were separated by punctuation (50 hits, 56.82% of the specific query hit count), where the context is almost exclusively related to political polarisation.

column14: The query covers very broad patterns, including the political division context. When reviewing the KWIC word forms, the most common KWIC form is "naše in vaše" with a frequency of 127 (or 1.55% of the original query result), with other forms of naš-vaš following (e.g. vaše in naše, naše, vaše), which is still relatively common given the broad KWIC pattern. The term usually refers to political division and is mostly used for direct or indirect attacks on the opposition (e.g., "Vi pa tudi premislite ali se splača uničevati prihodnost vaših in naših otrok , kakorkoli želite" / "You should also think about whether it is worth destroying your and our children's future, however you want.").

column14: A brief manual check of the hits revealed that the context of most terms refers to the clear naši-vaši polarisation, which was further suggested by a collocation check. Furthermore, the hit distribution trends show that this term (and its forms) was sparsely used in the years 1990 - 2000 (which was verified by an additional concordance check with a period filter covering only 1990 - 1990) with only 57 hits, where the context was not so much focused on the political divide, but rather neutral/non-political uses of the words.

column12: •Topic/genre: The concordance is most common in the text within the “Prosti čas” (Leisure, 1,885, 9.930, 152.42%) genre, followed by “Science” category (1,212, 12.31, 187.65%). However, looking at relative fr. In TT and relative density, the concordance seems to be most typical of the Education category (601, 17.92, 275.81%), however, it is important to highlight, that there is a lot of repeating texts that inflate these calculations.
column13: kalkulator (calculator), delitev (division), navigacija (navigation), hvala (thank you), izračunati (calculate), obsotelj (Obsotelj), zasebnost (privacy), pretvoriti (to convert), analizirati (to analyse), mladost (youth)
column14: Most texts with identifiable patterns fall under the "Leisure" category, in which terms related to audience engagement (e.g. naši/vaši bralci, Naša ekipa pride na vašo lokacijo...) or advertising language (e.g. Naši strokovnjaki bodo poskrbeli za vaša vprašanja...) appear. Some texts also touch on division, typically political. The texts in the "Education" category are more representative in terms of relative frequency. Surprisingly, "Politics" is less present, and where it does appear, the contexts vary. This is also reflected in the collocations — only one politically charged lemma ranks highly, while others refer to non-political contexts. One explanation lies in the bias of the data: repetitive articles on different websites (often with different category tags) and frequent concordance repetitions, such as adverts within articles (e.g. "Izračunajte si vašo plačo z našim kalkulatorjem"/"Calculate your salary with our calculator") or recurring website taglines ("Naša prihodnost bo tudi vaša"/"Our future will be yours too"  asking readers to donate) and privacy notices  (e.g. "Poleg tega analiziramo informacije o vaši uporabi našega spletnega mesta" / "In addition, we analyse information about your use of our website" ), inflate certain concordances and categories.

column14: Many of the observations, especially the concerns about the repetition of cross-posted articles and segments, also apply to the semi-broad pattern. However, as the pattern narrows to a one-token distance between words, the contexts become more concentrated. A prominent example is the phrase "Pijača vaše in naše mladosti" ("The drink of your and our youth"), which reflects both advertising language and nostalgic, collective memory. The  frequent collocation is the lemma delitev (division), which is directly tied to the context of political polarisation (naši–vaši).

column14: The majority of hits in this specific query refer to political polarisation and various forms of binary divisions (e.g., "Cepilci, anticepilci, komunisti, protestniki, begunci, politiki, kulturniki, geji, uradniki, naši, vaši , njihovi; vsi s(m)o zločinci!"/ "Waxers, anti-waxers, communists, protesters, refugees, politicians, culturists, gays, officials, ours, yours, theirs; they/we are all criminals!" ). The repetition of hits (due to cross-posted articles) is also present here, but much more limited. Manual check of all instances, where the words "naši" and "vaši" are separated (73 hits, 43.98% of specific query hit count) with a punctuation revealed that this form refers exclusively to political (and other binary) divisions. Examples:  "Ni lepše upodobljene delitve na vaši : naši" ("There is no more clearly portrayed division between 'yours' and 'ours'."), "...popredalčkanih razredov: naši-vaši , janšisti, fašisti in tako dalje" ("..of neatly labeled groups: us vs. them, Janšists, fascists, and the like")

column14: Most of the texts containing the queried expression belong to the "Promotion" domain, often use direct address and persuasive language ("Naši strokovnjaki vam bodo z veseljem pomagali, vam svetovali in odgovorili na vaša vprašanja."/"Our experts will gladly assist you, offer advice, and answer your questions."). Other relevant domains include "Legal", where formal wording is used to communicate obligations or rights (e.g. in relation to service notices), and "Opinion/Argumentation", where there are some parts that also refer to the polarising concept of vaš-naš ("To je naše mnenje o predlaganih ukrepih - kakšno je pa vaše?/"This is our opinion on the proposed measures – and what is yours?"). This subdivision is conceptually present, but not dominant in frequency: vaš-naš and related lemmas do not appear among the top ten collocations.

In this narrower context, the polarizing concept of naši/vaši ("ours/yours") becomes much clearer, while in broader samples it was diluted by the high proportion of advertising content.  In contrast to other corpora, manual check of the pattern naši[punctuation]vaši (200 hits, 40% of the specific query hit count) has shown that the term is not used exclusively in a polarising context, but also refers to our/your children.

column14: The concordance appears in various contexts, ranging from (political) polarisation, corporate advertising (e.g. " 60 let pijače naše in vaše mladosti, #cockta #bambus" / "60 years of the drink of our and your youth, #cockta #bambus"), to the concordance appearing in the title of a performance ("Naše nasilje in vaše nasilje") extracted from KWIC word forms and collocations (which, however, have a very low number of co-occurrences.). In terms of frequency, this concordance is very present in tweets written by private users, while in terms of relative frequency, this concordance is very prominent within the corporate tweets. Furthermore, based on the text type analysis, this concordance appears in languages other than Slovenian - Serbo-Croatian (hbs), English and undisclosed language, where it is true that this concordance is also present in Serbo-Croatian tweets, but these numbers are not accurate as some tweets are incorrectly annotated (through a manual check, we can confirm the minor presence of this concordance in Serbo-Croatian, but not in English or any other language).

column14: Although the number of hits decreases by almost 50 %, the remaining occurrences mainly relate to the polarisation between "ours" and "yours". Nevertheless, traces of advertising language remain, which is especially notable in the recurring formula "XY vaše in naše mladosti" ("XY of your and our youth") or in references to shared values, such as "the future of your and our children". As in the broader query, concordance is more frequent in private tweets in terms of raw frequency, while corporate tweets show a slightly higher relative frequency (3.98 vs. 3.93). However, this difference is minimal. The observations regarding the languages in which concordance occurs are consistent with the broader query: It occurs mainly in Slovenian, with a minor confirmed presence in Serbo-Croatian and no verified instances in English or other languages, due to some inconsistencies in the annotations.

column14: Looking at the specific form of the query, the context is predominantly linked to polarisation, as in examples such as: "Full je lažje biti naš kot vaš. Kervaši niso naši. #slonebuloze" ("It’s way easier to be one of us than one of you. Because yours aren’t ours. #slonebuloze"). The concordance also appears in discussions about various binary divisions, such as "naši/vaši = pokvarjeni/nepokvarjeni, komiji / oni drugi or levi/desni" ("ours/yours = corrupt/not corrupt, communists/the other side, left/right"). This emphasis on opposition is reflected in the distribution between private and corporate tweets, where concordance appears primarily in private tweets, both in terms of raw and relative frequency. Manual check of filtered naši[punctuation]vaši pattern suggests that this pattern refers almost exclusively to political polarisation and other forms of binary division (85 hits, 54,49% of specific query hit count). This is evident in the distribution across tweet types, where the concordance occurs almost exclusively in private tweets, both in terms of raw counts and relative frequency with only eight corporate tweets containing this pattern, all of which also refer to the naši/vaši (ours/theirs) divide and broader polarizing discourse.

column14:
An analysis of the hit distributions shows that in terms of raw frequency,the majority of texts containing the concordance can be found in the Tweet group, followed by the Forum texts. However, looking at the relative frequency, the concordance pattern is most prominent in forum texts (e.g. medover.net). Most concordance lines relate to (political) divisions, though there are also notable exceptions. One such example is a repetitive forum heading: "Prosimo, da s seboj prinesete natisnjena vaša vprašanja in naše odgovore" ("Please bring printed copies of your questions and our answers"), which accounts for 861 hits, which is 22.38% of all hits in the original query, significantly inflating the total count. Since the Janes Tweet corpus is a subset of the broader Janes social media corpus, the very common ad phrase following the pattern "XY of your and our youth" also appears here, contributing to the visibility of the concordance in this group. Manual check of texts across other groups (e.g. blogs, news, wiki) shows that expressions of naši/vaši occur particularly frequently in news and blog texts, especially in the comment sections of news. These often refer to ideological divisions and historical or political conflicts, including references to war (partizani/domobranci, beli in rdeči), and in some cases contain explicit threats or violent rhetoric, such as: "Dokler bodo vaše rakete pobijale naše ljudi, bodo naši noži rezali vratove vaših ljudi." ("As long as your rockets kill our people, our knives will cut your people's throats.") or: "Očistil bom ulico, našo in vašo!" ("I will clean up the street — ours and yours!")
There are also sports-related divisions ar, such as: "Naša liga je dost močnejša od vaše..." ("Our league is much stronger than yours..."). Due to several, repeating phrases, we calculated collocations with those phrases removed (n=2955), but some repetition is still present due to repetition of words vaš/naš within different hits (e.g. Učitelj, ki je izučil vaše starše, vam bo razkazal lepoto našega jezika, zaklade naše kulture, bogastvo naše vzgoje) .

column14: While texts from the Tweet group remain the most frequent in terms of raw frequency, the concordance appears to be most prominent in the News group when measured by relative frequency (density within text type). These news texts originate from three main platforms—RTV SLO, Mladina and Reporter — with the pattern particularly concentrated in Mladina and Reporter, both of which show a notably higher relative frequency. Due to the more narrowly defined query, many of the repetitive texts that appeared in broader patterns are no longer included. Tweets promoting nostalgic objects such as music or drinks (glasba/pijača ... naše in vaše mladosti, "music/drink of our and your youth"), however, still appear occasionally. The remaining concordance lines now more clearly reflect a polarizing discourse, which is also evident in the top 10 collocations, where terms closely associated with ideological division and rhetorical opposition dominate.

column14: The concordance concentrates mainly on the polarizing use of the words naši/vaši. It is noticeable that in many instances the words are separated by punctuation marks such as “-”, “:” or “/”. In order to check whether this formulation consistently reflects the polarizing concept, we filtered the results accordingly. The filtered subset comprises 112 hits, which corresponds to 52.83% of the original specific CQL query — and in almost all cases, the wording does indeed refer to the distinction between "ours" and "your".
